coredata.dc:title: An ultrasensitive electroanalytical sensor based on MgO/SWCNTs- 1-Butyl-3-methylimidazolium bis(trifluoromethylsulfonyl)imide paste electrode for the determination of ferulic acid in the presence sulfite in food samples
coredata.dc:creator: Zabihpour, Tahereh
Shahidi, Seyed-Ahmad
Karimi-Maleh, Hassan
Ghorbani-HasanSaraei, Azade
coredata.dc:description: This research focused on fabrication of an ultrasensitive nano-molar voltammetric sensor for the determination of ferulic acid (FA) in the presence of sulfite in food samples. For this goal, we incorporated MgO/SWCNTs nanocomposite and 1-Butyl-3-methylimidazolium bis(trifluoromethylsulfonyl)imide [Bmim][Tf2N] into the carbon paste matrix to yield the modified CPE (MgO/SWCNTs-[Bmim][Tf2N]-CPE) as an electroanalytical tool. The MgO/SWCNTs-[Bmim][Tf2N]-CPE produced an improved analytical oxidation signal for FA which was 3 0.17 times more than the unmodified sensors. The electrode was found to produce distinct electro-oxidation signals for FA ΔEp=300 mV in the presence of sulfite with, which proves its applicability for the determination of FA in the presence of sulfite ions commonly present in food samples (e.g. red wine). The MgO/SWCNTs-[Bmim][Tf2N]-CPE proved to be a powerful tool for the determination of FA and sulfite ions (recovery data between 97.84%-103.1%) in the respective concentration ranges of 0.009–450 µM and 0.1–450 µM with detection limit of 3.0 nM and 50 nM, respectively. In the final step, the MgO/SWCNTs-[Bmim][Tf2N]-CPE was successfully used in the determination of FA and sulfite in food samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ferulic acid
S;ulfite, MgO/SWCNTs nanocomposite
1-Butyl-3-methylimidazolium bis(trifluoromethylsulfonyl)imide
Food electrochemical sensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Estimation of FA and sulfite in food samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FESEM image (A); XRD pattern (B) and EDS analysis data of MgO/SWCNTs.
Linear diagram of Epa vs. pH for electro-oxidation of 120 µM FA. DP voltammograms of 120 µM FA in the pH range 3.5–5.5 at surface of MgO/SWCNTs-[Bmim][Tf2N]-CPE.

DP voltammograms of 120 µM FA at surface of (a) CPE; (b) MgO/SWCNTs-CPE; c) [Bmim][Tf2N]-CPE and d) MgO/SWCNTs-[Bmim][Tf2N]-CPE. Insert) Current density data relative to electro-oxidation of 120 µM FA at surface of different electrodes.

Linear diagram of Ipa vs. ν1/2 for electro-oxidation of 600 µM FA at surface of MgO/SWCNTs-[Bmim][Tf2N]-CPE. Linear sweep voltammogram of 600 µM FA at scan rates (a) 10; (b) 30; (c) 60; (d) 90 and e) 150 mV/s.
A) Chronoamperograms a) 300 µM, b) 400 µM and c) 500 µM FA at surface of MgO/SWCNTs-[Bmim][Tf2N]-CPE at pH 4.5. B) Cottrell's plot for the data from the chronoamperograms.

(A) DPVs of MgO/SWCNTs-[Bmim][Tf2N]-CPE containing different concentrations of FA and sulfite (from a-e) mixed solutions of: (a) 65.0 + 30.0; (b) 80.0 + 35.0; (c) 100.0 + 45.0; (d) 140.0 + 70.0 and (e) 165.0 + 85.0 μM FA and sulfite, respectively. (B and C) Plots of the oxidation current as a function of FA and sulfite concentration, respectively.
The plot of oxidation current of 120 µM FA recorded in the different days at same condition.
Comparison of the analytical performance of MgO/SWCNTs-[Bmim][Tf2N]-CPE with reported FA electroanalytical sensors.
Investigation the ability of MgO/SWCNTs-[Bmim][Tf2N]-CPE for the determination of FA and sulfite in real samples.

row.entry: Founded sulfite by voltammetric published method (μM)

head.ce:title: An ultrasensitive electroanalytical sensor based on MgO/SWCNTs- 1-Butyl-3-methylimidazolium bis(trifluoromethylsulfonyl)imide paste electrode for the determination of ferulic acid in the presence sulfite in food samples

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: School of Resources and Enviroment, University of Electronic Science and Technology of China, P.O. Box 611731, Xiyuan Ave, Chengdu, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: University of Electronic Science and Technology of China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This research focused on fabrication of an ultrasensitive nano-molar voltammetric sensor for the determination of ferulic acid (FA) in the presence of sulfite in food samples. For this goal, we incorporated MgO/SWCNTs nanocomposite and 1-Butyl-3-methylimidazolium bis(trifluoromethylsulfonyl)imide [Bmim][Tf2N] into the carbon paste matrix to yield the modified CPE (MgO/SWCNTs-[Bmim][Tf2N]-CPE) as an electroanalytical tool. The MgO/SWCNTs-[Bmim][Tf2N]-CPE produced an improved analytical oxidation signal for FA which was 3 0.17 times more than the unmodified sensors. The electrode was found to produce distinct electro-oxidation signals for FA ΔEp=300 mV in the presence of sulfite with, which proves its applicability for the determination of FA in the presence of sulfite ions commonly present in food samples (e.g. red wine). The MgO/SWCNTs-[Bmim][Tf2N]-CPE proved to be a powerful tool for the determination of FA and sulfite ions (recovery data between 97.84%-103.1%) in the respective concentration ranges of 0.009–450 µM and 0.1–450 µM with detection limit of 3.0 nM and 50 nM, respectively. In the final step, the MgO/SWCNTs-[Bmim][Tf2N]-CPE was successfully used in the determination of FA and sulfite in food samples.

ce:section.ce:para: The analytical sensors used in food industry are important tools for the determination of levels of food additives and investigation of food quality, which is very important in human safety [1,2]. Traditionally the quality of the new products should be checked before introduction to the market [3]. Various analytical tools such as HPLC with different detectors [4–6] and UV–Vis spectroscopy [7] have been used in food industry. Yet they present different problems including the application of toxic solvents, low selectivity (in the case of UV–Vis spectroscopy), high cost and operation times, which has created interest in the area of designing new powerful sensors for such analyses [8,9].

Electroanalytical sensors have proliferated in the analysis of food ingredients during the past years [10–19]. Due to their high diversity, low cost and portability, these sensors quickly substituted the old ways of analysis in the industry [20–29]. The diversity in the modification process, application of different types of electrodes fabricated for selective analysis of different species are among the strengths of these devices [30-39]. On the other hand, the electrochemical sensors could be used for simultaneous determination of two or three different species (e.g. food additives) in short times and for low costs [40–45]. DNA, enzymatic and nanostructure-based sensors have recently been suggested for the analysis of biological, environmental and food additives with low limit of detection in the recent years [46–54]. In addition, application of SWCNTs with high conductivity and easy preparation compare of other conductive materials such as graphene oxide helps to improvement conductivity and selectivity of electrochemical sensors [55].

Further, the application of mediators in the fabrication of electrochemical sensors allows for the selective and sensitive measurement of electroactive compounds [56–61]. As an example, Tahernejad-Javazmia et al. fabricated a highly sensitive electrochemical sensor using 3D reduced graphene oxide/FeNi3 as a conductive powder, and n-hexyl-3-methylimidazolium hexafluoro phosphate as conductive binders for the analysis of tert‑butylhydroquinone and folic acid in food samples at nano-molar detection limit [13].

Ferulic acid and sulfite are two major food antioxidant additives that used in different types of food products such as red wine. Although these antioxidants have many effects on the shelf life of foods, their overdose can lead to potential allergy problems. Therefore, design new type of electroanalytical sensors with fast analysis time is very important to check for food.

A thorough review of the literature shows that the ability for the design and fabrication of new sensors indicates that there is an on-going need for the development of these devices and hence the present work was focused on the development of an ultrasensitive and selective analytical sensor for the determination of ferulic acid in the presence of sulfite. This is first electroanalytical sensor that fabricated for simultaneous determination of ferulic acid in the presence of sulfite.

The resulting MgO/SWCNTs-[Bmim][Tf2N]-CPE showed enhanced limit of detection and linear dynamic range in comparison to other electrochemical sensors reported for this purpose (see Table 1 )[62–67]. MgO/SWCNTs-[Bmim][Tf2N]-CPE was also used for the determination of FA in the presence of sulfite ions (as two important food additives) and a ΔEp of 300 mV was observed. The modified CPE was hence concluded as a new analytical sensor for determination of FA and sulfite in food samples especially red wine.

A Metrohm 910 PSTAT voltammetric analyzer was used for the analyses and the voltammetric signals were recorded using Ag|AgCl|KClsat as reference electrode and Pt wire as counter electrode. The XRD analyses on the MgO/SWCNTs composite were performed on an ARL PERFORMIR´X instrument.

Ferulic acid, sodium sulfite, graphite powder and 1-Butyl-3-methylimidazolium bis(trifluoromethylsulfonyl)imide were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Co. Phosphoric acid was purchased from Acros Co. and was used for preparing a 0.1 M phosphate buffer solution (PBS). The magnesium nitrate, sodium hydroxide and SWCNTs were obtained from Merck Company.

In this first step, 1.5 g of COOH functionalized SWCNTs was dispersed in 50 mL of 0.5 M NaOH under stirring for 20 min and then ultra-sonicated for 10 min at 400 W. After dispersing of COOH functionalized SWCNTs in to water solution, the 50 mL of a magnesium nitrate solution was added, in a drop-wise manner, to the vessel over 45 min. In this section, the Mg(OH)2 was precipitated at surface of COOH functionalized SWCNTs. The black precipitate formed was then filtered off and the washed with an ethanol: water solution seven times. In the final step, the black powder was dried for 18 h, at 120 °C and then calcined at 500 °C for 2.5 h for converting Mg(OH)2/ SWCNTs to MgO/SWCNTs.

The MgO/SWCNTs-[Bmim][Tf2N]-CPE was prepared and later used as the working electrode through the below procedure. The graphite powder /MgO/SWCNTs ratio was optimized by recording of oxidation signal of a 120 µM FA solution using electrodes with various compositions in differential pulse voltammetry analyses. By going from an unmodified CPE to a CPE modified with 8% (w:w) of MgO/SWCNTs, the oxidation signal of increased but was improved more (Fig. S1; Supplementary information data). Furthermore, adding the ionic liquid in the binder of the CPE upto 20% (v:v), also enhanced the oxidation signal of FA. Above this value, however, the oxidation signal of FA decreased, which was attributed to the formation of an inactive electrode surface with a layer of [Bmim][Tf2N] (see figure S2; supplementary information data).

Therefore, the MgO/SWCNTs-[Bmim][Tf2N]-CPE was prepared by mixing 920 g graphite powder; and 80 mg of MgO/SWCNTs using paraffin and [Bmim][Tf2N] at a ratio of 8:2 v:v as the binder. The resultant MgO/SWCNTs-[Bmim][Tf2N]-CPE was immediately packed into a tube from one end, while a copper wire was inserted into the paste from the other end to establish an electric contact.

Red wine was centrifuged for 10 min at 2000 rpm and then filtered. The resulting solution was then directly subjected to determination. For this purpose, 2.0 g of white rice were treated with a 50% ethanol solution for 30 min and then dissolved into 50 mL sulfuric acid. Next, the solution was sonicated for 2.5 h and then filtered using Whatman paper (No. 1).

FESEM was used as powerful [68] technique for characterizing the surface of the MgO/SWCNTs nanocomposite and the results are presence in Fig. 1 A. As can be seen, MgO nanoparticles are decorated on the surface of the SWCNTS tubes. In addition, the 022 miller index in XRD pattern of MgO/SWCNTs nanocomposite (Fig. 1B) confirms the presence of SWCNTs in the structure of the composite. On the other hand, the miller indices (111); (200); (220); (311) and (222) in the XRD pattern confirm the presence of spherical MgO nanoparticle (JCPDS card No: 78–0430) with crystalline structure. The particle size of the MgO nanoparticle was calculated to be 35.2 nm using the Debye-Scherrer's equation. On the other hand, the EDS analysis data confirm the presence of C, Mg and O elements for synthesize MgO/SWCNTs (Fig. 1C).

Due to the phenolic structure of FA and in the light of the literature, pH was taken as an effective and important factor in the electrochemical analysis of FA and was hence optimized by recording the DP voltammograms of 120 µM FA in the range of 3.5–5.5 (Fig. 2 inset). Monitoring the oxidation signals of FA in this pH range showed that the strongest oxidation signals occurred in pH=4.5, and subsequently under this condition the lowest concentrations of FA could be determined using this method. Therefore, the pH=4.5 was chosen as optimal value for the analysis of FA using MgO/SWCNTs-[Bmim][Tf2N]-CPE. The relation between the oxidation potential of FA and pH could be useful for investigation of the electrooxidation mechanism at the surface of MgO/SWCNTs-[Bmim][Tf2N]-CPE. The negative shift observed in the oxidation potential of differential pulsed voltammograms of FA, moving from pH=3.5 to pH=5.5 follows the equation:
(1)
E pa
= 0.0572
pH + 0.8579
This confirms that the electro-oxidation of FA occurs with an equal number of electrons and protons (Fig. 2).

After optimizing the pH, the modification process of CPE with MgO/SWCNTs and [Bmim][Tf2N] was investigated by recording DP voltammograms of 120 µM FA solution at pH=4.5 (Fig. 3 ). The oxidation of FA at the surface of CPE showed a low oxidation signal with a current of 6.5 µA at an oxidation potential of 0.67 V (curve a). After incorporating MgO/SWCNTs in the CPE composition the oxidation signal of FA shifted to 0.64 V and the oxidation current increased to 13.9 µA (curve b). After further modification of CPE with [Bmim][Tf2N] ([Bmim][Tf2N]-CPE), the oxidation signal of FA was observed at 0.63 V with oxidation current of 16.3 µA (curve c). Based on the results, it could be concluded that the presence of [Bmim][Tf2N] and MgO/SWCNTs enhance the conductivity of the modified CPE and helps improve the oxidation signal of FA. Therefore, the MgO/SWCNTs-[Bmim][Tf2N]-CPE was concluded as a modified electrode and was used to record the oxidation signal of FA. The oxidation signal of FA occurred at 0.59 V with an oxidation current of 20.4 µA (curve d). This means a decrease in the potential (~80 mV) and an increase in the current (~3.15 times) after amplification with MgO/SWCNTs and [Bmim][Tf2N].

The active surface area of the MgO/SWCNTs-[Bmim][Tf2N]-CPE, [Bmim][Tf2N]-CPE, MgO/SWCNTs-CPE and unmodified CPE were determined to be ~0.14 cm2, 0.263 cm2, 0.278 cm2 and 0.293 cm2. Using the active surface area values of different electrodes, the current densities of the electrodes were determined and results are illustrated in the inset of Fig. 3. As can be seen, the current density of MgO/SWCNTs-[Bmim][Tf2N]-CPE was more than other electrodes confirming the good electrical conductivity after modification.

The effects of the scan rate on the oxidation current and potential of FA were investigated (Fig. 4 inset)). The linear relation between the FA current and ν1/2 (Ipa = 1.6045 ν1/ 2+ 3.4677 (R2=0.9913)) (Fig. 4) complies with a diffusion-controlled mechanism for the electro-oxidation of FA at the surface of MgO/SWCNTs-[Bmim][Tf2N]-CPE.

The Tafel plot of MgO/SWCNTs-[Bmim][Tf2N]-CPE in the presence of 700 µM FA is presented in figure S3. Using the slope of this line and Tafel equation, the value of α was determine as ~0.66, which confirms the deviation from the reversible state.

Study of the effect of the scan rate further supports the diffusion-controlled mechanism for the electro-oxidation of FA at MgO/SWCNTs-[Bmim][Tf2N]-CPE. The value of diffusion coefficient (D) of FA was determined by recording chronoamperograms of 300 µM, 400 µM and 500 µM solution of the analyte at optimum conditions (Fig. 5 A). Based on these experiments the mean value of D was determined to be ~6.0 × 10−5 cm2 using the slopes of Cottrell plots (Fig. 5B).

Differential pulse voltammograms (DPVs) for different FA solutions (0.009 µM to 450.0 µM) in the presence of sulfite ion (0.1 to 450.0 µM) were recorded at MgO/SWCNTs-[Bmim][Tf2N]-CPE in pH 4.5 (Fig. S3 and Fig. S4). The respective linear equations, Ip = 0.1757 CFA + 0.1728 (R2 = 0.9951) and Ip = 0.2749 Csulfite + 2.4619 (R2 = 0.9973) were obtained for the calibration plots of FA and sulfite, and the respective limits of detection (LOD) were calculated to be 3.0 nM and 50.0 nM for FA and sulfite at 3S/M. This value of detection limit and linear dynamic range for FA is comparable and better than previous reported electroanalytical sensors (see Table 1).

FA and sulfite are two important antioxidants present in foods like red wine. Therefore, simultaneous determination of these additives are very important. The ability of the MgO/SWCNTs-[Bmim][Tf2N]-CPE for the determination of FA in the presence of sulfite was hence evaluated. The DP voltamogarms of mixed FA/sulfite solutions showed two separated oxidation signal with an ΔEp of 300 at surface of MgO/SWCNTs-[Bmim][Tf2N]-CPE. This separated potential for two compound is sufficient for simultaneous determination of them in real smaples.

Further DP voltammograms of different concentration of FA and sulfite were recorded using the modified electrode (Fig. 6 A). The sensitivity of the method for FA and sulfite were determined to be 0.1760 µA/µM and 0.2733 µA/µM, respectively (Fig. 6B and C), which were similar with the sensitivity obtained for FA and sulfite in the dynamic range experiments, confirming the capability of MgO/SWCNTs-[Bmim][Tf2N]-CPE for the analysis of FA in the presence of sulfite.

DP voltammograms of 120 µM FA were recorded on the different days using the MgO/SWCNTs-[Bmim][Tf2N]-CPE (Fig. 7 inset). After ten, twenty and thirty days, 96.5%, 93.4% and 91.0% of the initial signal were still obtainable confirming the stability of the modified electrode (Fig. 7). The selectivity's of the MgO/SWCNTs-[Bmim][Tf2N]-CPE to FA and sulfite were checked by recording the DP voltammograms of these analytes in the absence and in the presence of interfering compounds and an acceptable error level of 5% was observed. The obtained results are presented in table S1, confirming the good selectivity of MgO/SWCNTs-[Bmim][Tf2N]-CPE for the determination of FA and sulfite.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Estimation of FA and sulfite in food samples
ce:section.ce:para: The preparation of red wine and white rice has been prepared described in Section 2.4. The obtained data were checked with those in other reports and recovery studies were carried out using DPV analysis and the results were found to be satisfying for FA and sulfite (Table 2 ). As can be seen, the recovery data between 97.84%−103.1% were detected for determination of these food additive using MgO/SWCNTs-[Bmim][Tf2N]-CPE as electrochemical sensor that confirm high performance ability of fabricated sensor.

The presence work focused on design and fabrication of new electroanalytical food sensor. Therefore, an ultrasensitive electrochemical sensor was fabricated through modifying a carbon paste electrode with two highly conductive mediators. The MgO/SWCNTs nanocomposite was synthesized by a simple method and results showed diameter ~35.2 nm using the Debye-Scherrer's equation. MgO/SWCNTs-[Bmim][Tf2N]-CPEs were prepared and used for the determination of FA in the presence of sulfite in different food samples with recovery data between 97.84%−103.1% that is acceptable for a high performance analytical tool. The MgO/SWCNTs-[Bmim][Tf2N]-CPE showed detection limits of 3.0 nM and 50.0 nM for of FA and sulfite and was hence concluded as a novel electroanalytical tool for the determination of these analytes in different food samples.

Tahereh Zabihpour: Data curation, Formal analysis. Seyed-Ahmad Shahidi: Investigation, Methodology, Project administration. Hassan Karimi-Maleh: Validation, Visualization, Writing - original draft. Azade Ghorbani-HasanSaraei: Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: There is not any conflict interest in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.microc.2019.104572.
